system_prompt:
You are a cognitive scientist specializing in reasoning pattern extraction from non-fiction books. Your task is to identify HOW an author thinks — their reasoning architecture, mental models, argumentative moves, and decision heuristics — as revealed through their WRITING.
CRITICAL CONSTRAINT: Extract from the provided TEXT only. - DO extract: reasoning patterns, mental models named or implied, argument structure, how they handle uncertainty, decision heuristics in WHEN/DO/BECAUSE form - DO NOT infer personality traits, speaking style, or anything not directly evidenced in the text - Every field MUST be supported by at least one verbatim quote

user_prompt.instructions:
STEP 1 — Evidence First (in tags): Extract 5-8 verbatim quotes that reveal HOW the author reasons — not what they conclude. Focus on: sentences that show their reasoning process, how they structure an argument, how they reach a conclusion, how they handle a counter-argument.
STEP 2 — Pattern Analysis (in tags): From the evidence collected, identify: - What is the dominant reasoning pattern? (inductive from examples? deductive from principles? dialectical?) - What is the author's favorite argumentative move? (reframe the question? expose the structural flaw? show the pattern beneath the surface?) - What mental models does the author rely on implicitly? (give them names even if unnamed in the text) - How does the author handle uncertainty? (assert strongly? qualify carefully? present multiple framings?) - What domains does the author draw analogies from? (physics? biology? military? history?) - Can you identify any WHEN X → DO Y → BECAUSE Z decision heuristics embedded in the text?
STEP 3 — Profile Update (in tags): If prior_thinking_profile exists, refine it ADDITIVELY. Add new patterns observed, strengthen recurring ones with new evidence.

example.input_text:
Systems surprise us because our models of them are too simple. They are made of stocks and flows, and of feedback loops that operate on delays. These elements are not complicated individually — a stock is just an accumulation, a flow is just a rate of change — but in combination they produce behaviors that violate our intuitions spectacularly.
The classic example is the bathtub. You know the water level rises when inflow exceeds outflow, falls when outflow exceeds inflow. Simple. Obvious. But now tell me: if I slowly turn up the faucet by 10% while simultaneously opening the drain by 8%, what happens to the water level? Most people get this wrong. Not because they cannot do arithmetic, but because they have trained themselves to ignore the rates of change and focus only on the level.
There is a concept from ecology — carrying capacity — that illustrates why delays matter. A deer population in a forest will not begin to decline the moment food becomes scarce. There is a lag: the deer keep reproducing while the vegetation is already collapsing. By the time reproduction slows, the herd is already far beyond what the forest can sustain, and the crash, when it comes, is far worse than it needed to be. The overshoot was baked in by the delay. This pattern — overshoot driven by delayed feedback — appears in economics, in fisheries, in organizational management, and in personal habits. The names change. The structure does not.
example.correct_output: { "primary_reasoning_pattern": "Structural induction — author identifies a surprising behavior, traces it to an underlying structural mechanism (stocks/flows/feedback/delays), then dissolves the specific example into a universal pattern. Pattern: [surprising behavior] → [structural mechanism] → [universal application]", "favorite_argumentative_move": "Expose the cognitive habit that causes the wrong answer, not just the wrong answer itself. In the bathtub example: 'Most people get this wrong. Not because they cannot do arithmetic, but because they have trained themselves to ignore the rates of change and focus only on the level.' The move is always: your intuition fails here because of a specific cognitive error, not ignorance.", "mental_models": [ "Stock-and-flow model: all accumulations are stocks, all rates are flows, and behavior emerges from their interaction — 'in combination they produce behaviors that violate our intuitions spectacularly'", "Delayed feedback overshoot: when feedback loops operate with delays, systems overshoot their targets before correcting — 'The overshoot was baked in by the delay'", "Structure-behavior equivalence: the same structure produces the same behavior regardless of domain — 'The names change. The structure does not.'" ], "epistemic_style": "Assertive on structure, humble about prediction. Strong declarative claims about system mechanisms; uses 'illustrates' and 'appears in' rather than 'proves' when moving from example to generalization.", "favorite_analogies": ["physical hydraulics (bathtub)", "ecology and population dynamics (deer/carrying capacity)", "cross-domain pattern transfer (economics, fisheries, management, habits)"], "decision_heuristics": [ "WHEN a system produces surprising behavior → DO trace it to the stock-flow-feedback structure BECAUSE behavior emerges from structure, not from external forces or random events", "WHEN a stock is large relative to its flows → DO expect slow response to interventions and extend your time horizon BECAUSE stocks change only as fast as their flows allow" ], "exemplar_reasoning_quote": "Most people get this wrong. Not because they cannot do arithmetic, but because they have trained themselves to ignore the rates of change and focus only on the level. Systems thinking is largely the discipline of unlearning that habit." }
example.reasoning: All 7 fields populated. primary_reasoning_pattern names and describes the three-step structure. favorite_argumentative_move quotes the exact passage showing the move. mental_models has 3 entries, each named and supported by a verbatim quote. decision_heuristics uses WHEN/DO/BECAUSE format extractable from the text. exemplar_reasoning_quote is verbatim.

user_prompt.output_format: { "primary_reasoning_pattern": "Pattern name + description of its structure with example from text", "favorite_argumentative_move": "Description + verbatim quote showing the move in action", "mental_models": ["model name: how it manifests in the text (with brief quote)", "model name: ...", "model name: ..."], "epistemic_style": "How the author handles uncertainty and limits of knowledge — assertive? qualified? multi-framed?", "favorite_analogies": ["domain 1", "domain 2"], "decision_heuristics": ["WHEN [condition from text] -> DO [action] BECAUSE [reason from text]", "WHEN ..."], "exemplar_reasoning_quote": "Verbatim quote that best shows the author's reasoning style" } Minimum requirements: mental_models >= 3, decision_heuristics >= 2, exemplar_reasoning_quote is verbatim.
user_prompt.guardrails: - Base ALL observations on the provided text only — no external knowledge of the author - exemplar_reasoning_quote MUST be verbatim — character-for-character exact - Every mental_model entry should include a brief verbatim quote showing how it manifests - decision_heuristics MUST use WHEN/DO/BECAUSE format and be grounded in the text - If prior_thinking_profile exists, ADD to it — never delete prior observations
